title: Maximum Subarray Sum After at Most K Swaps
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposals. All three use the same fixed-interval exchange argument; the runtime and memory variants change the order-statistic implementation for their respective objectives.

variants.languageReason: The constraints create O(n^2) candidate intervals with many rank, kth, and rank-sum queries. C++ gives native Fenwick loops, compact vectors, and predictable 64-bit arithmetic without managed-runtime overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It provides the strongest balance of native speed, concise coordinate compression, Fenwick trees, and LeetCode runner maturity.
Raw loops are competitive, but manual dynamic arrays and coordinate-compression plumbing add risk without reducing this variant's asymptotic memory.
Native and memory-safe, but mutable tree state and repeated rank queries are more verbose and typically have less favorable constants than the C++ version on LeetCode.
Compiled and straightforward, but bounds checks and runtime overhead are less attractive for millions of Fenwick probes.
Primitive arrays can be fast after JIT warmup, but C++ avoids GC/runtime baseline costs and keeps memory layout lean.
The window count and order-statistic query volume are too large for interpreter or VM-heavy solutions to target the top percentile.

variants.approachJustification: For each subarray, use Fenwick trees for the inside and outside multisets. The useful swap count is found by checking the two global thresholds around the inside/outside crossing, then Fenwick sums compute the gain.

variants.languageReason: The runtime variant is dominated by Fenwick probes and per-left initialization. C++ fixed arrays, memcpy, memset, and scalar totals minimize allocation and object overhead while preserving the same optimal interval enumeration.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Fixed arrays and inlined helpers give the lowest practical constants for the hinted data-structure approach.
Could match the array layout, but LeetCode's C++ vector input and standard sorting/compression keep the solution safer with equivalent native loops.
Native performance is possible, but checked indexing and borrow management around several mutable arrays make it harder to outpace the C++ hot path.
Slices are convenient, but bounds checks and runtime services add overhead to the dense Fenwick loops.
JIT can optimize primitive arrays, but C++ has lower startup and more predictable top-percentile behavior for this short dense workload.
Too many scalar tree operations for interpreted or VM-heavy implementations.

variants.approachJustification: Same fixed-interval exchange argument as the recommended solution, but the Fenwick trees are fixed arrays reused for every left endpoint.

variants.languageReason: The memory variant needs a compact mutable inside tree plus static global prefixes. C++ keeps those arrays lean and avoids managed-runtime or object-heavy numeric storage.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It minimizes live mutable order-statistic state while staying practical for LeetCode constraints.
Could reduce library baseline slightly, but it would not beat the O(n) compressed-value state and would make the implementation more fragile.
Strong memory control, but the complement-prefix order-statistic code is more cumbersome and does not improve the footprint.
Slice storage is simple but the runtime and GC baseline are larger than C++ for a memory objective.
Primitive arrays avoid boxing, but managed-runtime overhead is higher than C++.
Object-heavy numeric containers are dominated for memory use.

variants.approachJustification: Maintain only the inside Fenwick tree. Outside counts and sums are derived as global prefix totals minus inside prefix totals, trading an extra logarithm for fewer mutable arrays.

reasoningSummary: Fix an interval. For t swaps, the optimal gain is the sum of the t largest outside values minus the sum of the t smallest inside values. The paired marginal gains are non-increasing, and the positive-pair count is maximized at one of the two global thresholds around the inside/outside crossing. Enumerating intervals and maintaining compressed order-statistic sums gives an exact solution.
timeComplexity: O(n^2 log n) recommended/runtime; O(n^2 log^2 n) memory
